primary_diagnosis.name: Hidradenitis suppurativa (Hurley stage II/III), acute inflammatory flare

pathophysiology_summary: Hidradenitis suppurativa is a chronic auto-inflammatory disease of terminal hair follicles in apocrine-bearing flexural skin (axillae, groin), driven by follicular occlusion/rupture and a brisk immune response, aggravated by friction, smoking and obesity. The chronic architecture — recurrent deep nodules, interconnected draining sinus tracts, hypertrophic scarring and double-comedones in a bilateral flexural distribution — defines the diagnosis and distinguishes it from a simple primary staphylococcal abscess (which lacks chronic sinuses/double-comedones), from lymphogranuloma/lymphadenitis (deep node pathology) and from a ruptured epidermoid cyst (solitary). Crucially, blind incision & drainage of a diffuse HS flare is often inappropriate and ineffective (recurrence, worse scarring); the correct acute management is potent analgesia, systemic anti-inflammatory antibiotics (e.g., tetracyclines), consideration of intralesional corticosteroid for a dominant nodule, absorbent dressings/friction reduction, smoking cessation, and dermatology referral for long-term immunomodulation. The safety-net is for superimposed spreading cellulitis/sepsis, which is absent here (no rapidly advancing erythema, no high fever/rigors).

differential.evidence_basis: Recurrent flexural nodules with sinus tracts, scarring and double-comedones.
Can coexist/mimic, but the chronic sinuses/double-comedones define HS; blind I&D of diffuse HS is often inappropriate.
Deep node pathology rather than chronic superficial sinus tracts.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise HS (not a simple abscess), avoid inappropriate blind I&D, and treat with analgesia + anti-inflammatory antibiotics + referral; screen for superimposed cellulitis/sepsis.

differential.evidence_basis: Chronic HS architecture; no spreading cellulitis or systemic toxicity.
No rapidly advancing erythema, high fever or rigors — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: analgesia + systemic anti-inflammatory antibiotics + dressings/friction reduction + smoking cessation + dermatology referral; cellulitis/sepsis safety-net.

red_flags.description: Superimposed spreading cellulitis / sepsis (rapidly advancing hot erythema, high fever, rigors)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would need systemic antibiotics ± admission; HS flares can be complicated by true bacterial infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a diffuse HS flare as a simple abscess and performing/advising blind incision & drainage, worsening scarring and recurrence.
Framing HS as an infection and giving short antibiotic courses without anti-inflammatory dosing, dermatology referral or trigger management.
Not addressing smoking and friction, key modifiable drivers.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Assessing a dominant fluctuant nodule for possible drainage/intralesional steroid
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Provide analgesia and systemic anti-inflammatory antibiotics, absorbent dressings and friction reduction, smoking-cessation support and urgent dermatology referral; in-person review for any dominant fluctuant nodule; ED if spreading cellulitis/sepsis.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Advising blind I&D or treating purely as an infection, without HS-specific management.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: HS: use anti-inflammatory antibiotic regimens (e.g., tetracyclines), intralesional corticosteroids for acute nodules, address smoking/weight/friction, and refer for advanced immunomodulatory therapy; avoid simple I&D of diffuse disease.
